title: Physical Quantities and Measurement
subtitle: Chapter 1: Physical Quantities, Units, Dimensions and Measurement — Complete NEB Notes

content.text: 1. Physical Quantities
Physical Quantity: Those quantities which can be measured and expressed in numerical values with units are called physical quantities. Example: Mass, length, time, temperature, etc.

content.name: Physical Quantity Representation
content.note: Where Q = Physical quantity, N = Numerical value, U = Unit

content.text: 2. Types of Physical Quantities
1. Fundamental Quantities: Those physical quantities which do not depend on other quantities for their measurement. Also called base quantities.
Examples: Length, Mass, Time, Temperature, Electric Current, Luminous Intensity, Amount of Substance
2. Derived Quantities: Those physical quantities which depend on fundamental quantities for their measurement.
Examples: Area, Volume, Density, Velocity, Acceleration, Force, Energy, Power, etc.
3. Units
Unit: A standard quantity of the same kind chosen as a reference for measurement of a physical quantity is called a unit.
Examples: Meter (m), Second (s), Kilogram (kg), Newton (N), etc.
3.1 Types of Units
1. Fundamental Units: The units of fundamental quantities are called fundamental units.
Examples: m (meter), kg (kilogram), s (second), K (Kelvin), A (Ampere), cd (candela), mol (mole)
2. Derived Units: The units of derived quantities are called derived units.
Examples: m/s (velocity), m/s² (acceleration), N (force), J (energy), W (power), etc.
4. Systems of Units
1. FPS System (Foot-Pound-Second):
Length → Foot (ft), Mass → Pound (lb), Time → Second (s)
2. MKS System (Meter-Kilogram-Second):
Length → Meter (m), Mass → Kilogram (kg), Time → Second (s)
3. CGS System (Centimeter-Gram-Second):
Length → Centimeter (cm), Mass → Gram (g), Time → Second (s)
4.1 SI System (International System of Units)
SI System: The internationally accepted system of units is called the SI system. It was adopted in 1960.
Seven Fundamental SI Units:

1. Length → Meter (m) • 2. Mass → Kilogram (kg) • 3. Time → Second (s) • 4. Temperature → Kelvin (K) • 5. Electric Current → Ampere (A) • 6. Luminous Intensity → Candela (cd) • 7. Amount of Substance → Mole (mol)
Supplementary Units: • Plane Angle → Radian (rad) • Solid Angle → Steradian (sr)
5. Characteristics of Standard Units
1. Well-defined: Should be clearly defined and of suitable size
2. Reproducible: Should be easily reproducible at any place
3. Constant: Should not change with time and place
4. Universal: Should be universally accepted
5. Convenient: Should be convenient to use
6. Dimensions of Physical Quantities
Dimensions: The powers to which the fundamental units are raised to represent a physical quantity are called dimensions.
Dimensional Formula: An expression showing which fundamental units and with what powers appear in a physical quantity.
Notation: [M] = Mass, [L] = Length, [T] = Time, [K] = Temperature, [A] = Electric Current

7. Principle of Homogeneity of Dimensions
Principle: For a physical equation to be dimensionally correct, the dimensions of all terms on both sides of the equation must be the same.
Example: Check if s = ut + ½at² is dimensionally correct.

content.name: Solution
content.formula:
LHS: [s] = [L] = [M^0L^1T^0]

RHS: [ut] = [LT^{-1}][T] = [L]
[\frac{1}{2}at^2] = [LT^{-2}][T^2] = [L]

All terms have [L], so equation is dimensionally correct.

content.text: Example: Check if v² = u² + 2as is dimensionally correct.

content.name: Solution
content.formula:
LHS: [v^2] = [LT^{-1}]^2 = [L^2T^{-2}]

RHS: [u^2] = [LT^{-1}]^2 = [L^2T^{-2}]
[2as] = [LT^{-2}][L] = [L^2T^{-2}]

All terms have [L²T⁻²], so equation is dimensionally correct.

content.text: 8. Uses of Dimensional Analysis
1. To check the correctness of a physical equation
2. To derive relationships between physical quantities
3. To convert units from one system to another
8.1 Deriving Relations Using Dimensional Analysis
Example: Derive the dimensional relation for time period of a simple pendulum. It depends on length (l), mass (m), and acceleration due to gravity (g).

content.name: Solution - Step by Step
content.note: Time period is independent of mass!

content.text: Example: Derive the relation for centripetal force. It depends on mass (m), velocity (v), and radius (r).

content.name: Solution - Step by Step
content.formula:
Step 1: Let F \propto m^a v^b r^c
Or, F = k m^a v^b r^c

Step 2: Write dimensional formula:
[MLT^{-2}] = [M]^a [LT^{-1}]^b [L]^c

Step 3: Simplify:
[M^1L^1T^{-2}] = [M^a L^{b+c} T^{-b}]

Step 4: Equate powers:
For M: a = 1
For T: -b = -2 \Rightarrow b = 2
For L: b + c = 1 \Rightarrow 2 + c = 1 \Rightarrow c = -1

Step 5: Substitute values:
F = k m^1 v^2 r^{-1}
F = k \frac{mv^2}{r}

Experimentally, k = 1
\therefore F = \frac{mv^2}{r}
content.note: This is the formula for centripetal force

content.text: 8.2 Conversion of Units Using Dimensional Formula
Example: Convert 50 dyne to Newton.

content.name: Solution

content.text: Example: Convert 72 km/h to m/s.

content.name: Solution
content.note: To convert km/h to m/s, multiply by 5/18

content.text: 9. Limitations of Dimensional Analysis
1. Cannot determine dimensionless constants (like π, e, etc.)
2. Cannot derive relations involving trigonometric, exponential, or logarithmic functions
3. Difficult to derive relations when more than 3 physical quantities are involved
4. Cannot distinguish between scalar and vector quantities
5. A dimensionally correct equation may not be physically correct
10. Measurement and Errors
Measurement: The comparison of an unknown physical quantity with a standard known quantity of the same kind is called measurement.
True Value: The actual value of a physical quantity.
Observed Value: The value obtained from measurement.
Error: The difference between true value and observed value.

content.name: Error Formula
content.note: Error can be positive or negative

content.text: 10.1 Types of Errors
1. Systematic Errors:
Errors that occur due to faulty instruments, wrong technique, or personal bias. They are constant and can be corrected.
Examples: Zero error, calibration error, parallax error
2. Random Errors:
Errors that occur due to unpredictable fluctuations in experimental conditions. They vary in magnitude and sign.
Examples: Temperature fluctuations, voltage fluctuations, vibrations
11. Accuracy and Precision
Accuracy: The closeness of measured value to the true value. More accurate measurement has smaller error.
Precision: The closeness of repeated measurements to each other. It depends on the least count of the instrument.
Note: A measurement can be precise but not accurate, or accurate but not precise.
12. Significant Figures
Significant Figures: The meaningful digits in a measured or calculated quantity are called significant figures. They include all certain digits plus one uncertain digit.
Rules for Determining Significant Figures:
1. All non-zero digits are significant. (Example: 235 has 3 significant figures)
2. Zeros between non-zero digits are significant. (Example: 2005 has 4 significant figures)
3. Leading zeros (before first non-zero digit) are NOT significant. (Example: 0.0025 has 2 significant figures)
4. Trailing zeros in a number with decimal point ARE significant. (Example: 2.500 has 4 significant figures)
5. Trailing zeros in a number without decimal point are NOT significant. (Example: 2500 has 2 significant figures)
6. Exact numbers (counting numbers) have infinite significant figures.
12.1 Rules for Calculations with Significant Figures
For Addition/Subtraction:
The result should have the same number of decimal places as the number with the least decimal places.
Example: 12.5 + 3.24 = 15.74 → 15.7 (one decimal place)
For Multiplication/Division:
The result should have the same number of significant figures as the number with the least significant figures.
Example: 2.5 × 3.42 = 8.55 → 8.6 (2 significant figures)
13. Important Formulas Summary

content.label: 🎓 Exam Tips
content.text: • Dimensionless quantities: Angle, Strain, Refractive Index, Mechanical Advantage • Same dimensions: Work & Energy, Torque & Energy, Pressure & Stress • Remember common dimensional formulas: Force [MLT⁻²], Energy [ML²T⁻²], Power [ML²T⁻³] • For checking homogeneity, all terms must have same dimensions • To derive relations, assume powers and equate dimensions • 1 N = 10⁵ dyne • 1 J = 10⁷ erg • km/h to m/s: multiply by 5/18 • m/s to km/h: multiply by 18/5
14. Common Mistakes to Avoid

❌ Forgetting that constants (π, ½, 2, etc.) are dimensionless • ❌ Not equating powers correctly in dimensional analysis • ❌ Confusing accuracy with precision • ❌ Miscounting significant figures (especially zeros) • ❌ Forgetting that dimensionally correct ≠ physically correct • ❌ Not converting units before calculations • ❌ Assuming all quantities can be derived dimensionally • ❌ Forgetting to check homogeneity in equations
